content:
# Numbers Chapter 19 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 171–172; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 126–127 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_019_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_019_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE RED HEIFER AND THE WATERS OF PURIFICATION: DEATH'S CONTAMINATION AND ITS REMEDY:** Numbers 19 stands as one of the most enigmatic ritual texts in the Torah, presenting the preparation and application of the waters of lustration (מֵי נִדָּה, mei niddah) for purification from corpse-contamination. The chapter divides into two sections: the preparation of the purification-ash from a slaughtered red heifer (vv. 1–10), and the laws governing corpse-contamination and the sprinkling ritual (vv. 11–22). The paradox at the heart of the chapter is well attested in rabbinic literature: the one who prepares the purification substance becomes unclean, while the unclean person who receives it becomes clean. Every participant in the preparation — the priest who oversees the burning (v. 7), the one who burns the heifer (v. 8), the one who gathers the ashes (v. 10) — is rendered unclean until evening. The ritual requires a פָּרָה אֲדֻמָּה תְּמִימָה (parah adummah temimah), a "red heifer, perfect one" — whole, unblemished, upon which no yoke has come. Eleazar (not Aaron) officiates, and the burning occurs outside the camp. Cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet yarn are cast into the pyre — the same triad used in the purification of the leper (Lev 14:4–6). In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 19 begins on page 171 (right column, mid-page) and continues onto page 172. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the chapter across pages 126–127 (pa_0126 to pa_0127), with the chapter heading ΜΘ visible. The parashah marker פ (open section) precedes v. 1, marking this as the beginning of the parashah חֻקַּת (Khuqqat, "statute of").

---

## The Red Heifer and the Preparation of the Purification-Ash (vv. 1–10)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** The double address to both Moses and Aaron is significant; most Levitical legislation is addressed to Moses alone or to Moses for Aaron. Here both receive the command jointly, perhaps because the ritual involves priestly action (Eleazar) under Moses' civil authority. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων ("and [the] Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying").

**2** "This [zot] [is] the-statute of [khuqqat] the-Torah [ha-Torah] which [asher] commanded [tsivvah] YHWH, saying [le'mor]: Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-let-them-bring [ve-yiqkhu] unto-you [elekha] a-heifer [parah], red [adummah], perfect [temimah], which [asher] there-is-no [ein] in-her [bah] blemish [mum], which [asher] not [lo] has-gone-up [alah] upon-her [aleiha] a-yoke [ol].

> **Key terms:** חֻקַּת הַתּוֹרָה (khuqqat ha-Torah) = "the statute of the Torah" — an unusual compound phrase. חֻקָּה (khuqqah) typically denotes an inexplicable decree, a law whose rationale transcends human comprehension. The rabbis (b. Yoma 67b) classified the red heifer as the paradigmatic חֹק (khoq), a statute that defies rational explanation. פָּרָה אֲדֻמָּה (parah adummah) = "a red heifer" — the adjective אֲדֻמָּה (adummah, "red") is from the root אדם (a-d-m), the same root as אָדָם (adam, "human/Adam") and אֲדָמָה (adamah, "ground/earth"). תְּמִימָה (temimah) = "perfect/complete" — feminine of תָּמִים (tamim), used for sacrificial animals that must be without defect (Lev 1:3, 10; 3:1). אֲשֶׁר אֵין־בָּהּ מוּם (asher ein-bah mum) = "which has no blemish in her" — an additional specification beyond תְּמִימָה, emphasizing the absolute physical perfection required. אֲשֶׁר לֹא־עָלָה עָלֶיהָ עֹל (asher lo-alah aleiha ol) = "which a yoke has not gone up upon her" — the animal must never have been used for labor, preserving its status as entirely consecrated. **LXX:** αὕτη ἡ διαστολὴ τοῦ νόμου ... δάμαλιν πυρρὰν ἄμωμον ("this is the ordinance of the law ... a red heifer without blemish") — rendering פָּרָה as δάμαλιν ("young cow/heifer") and אֲדֻמָּה as πυρράν ("flame-red").

**3** And-you-shall-give [u-netattem] [et]-her [otah] unto [el] Eleazar [El'azar] the-priest [ha-kohen], and-he-shall-bring-out [ve-hotsi] [et]-her [otah] unto [el] the-outside of [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh], and-one-shall-slaughter [ve-shakhat] [et]-her [otah] before-him [lefanav].

> **Key terms:** אֶלְעָזָר הַכֹּהֵן (El'azar ha-kohen) = "Eleazar the priest" — Aaron's son and eventual successor (Num 20:26–28). The delegation to Eleazar rather than Aaron himself may indicate that this ritual, performed outside the camp, would render the officiant unclean — a status incompatible with the High Priest's duties at the sanctuary. אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה (el-mikhuts la-makhaneh) = "to the outside of the camp" — the heifer is removed from the sacred precinct, paralleling the sin offering whose blood is brought inside the sanctuary but whose body is burned outside the camp (Lev 4:12, 21; 16:27). וְשָׁחַט (ve-shakhat) = "and-one-shall-slaughter" — the subject is unspecified (third person), suggesting a non-priestly slaughterer; Eleazar oversees but does not necessarily perform the killing. **LXX:** καὶ δώσεις αὐτὴν πρὸς Ελεαζαρ τὸν ἱερέα, καὶ ἐξάξουσιν αὐτὴν ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς ... καὶ σφάξουσιν αὐτὴν ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ ("and you shall give her to Eleazar the priest, and they shall bring her out outside the camp ... and they shall slaughter her before him").

**4** And-shall-take [ve-laqakh] Eleazar [El'azar] the-priest [ha-kohen] from-her-blood [mi-damah] with-his-finger [be-etsba'o], and-shall-sprinkle [ve-hizzah] toward [el] the-face of [nokhakh penei] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed] from-her-blood [mi-damah] seven [sheva] times [pe'amim].

> **Key terms:** בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ (be-etsba'o) = "with his finger" — the same manual application as on Yom Kippur, when the High Priest sprinkles blood with his finger upon the kapporet (Lev 16:14). אֶל־נֹכַח פְּנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (el-nokhakh penei Ohel Mo'ed) = "toward the face of the Tent of Meeting" — since the ritual takes place outside the camp, the sprinkling is directed toward the sanctuary from a distance, maintaining a ritual connection to the sacred center. שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים (sheva pe'amim) = "seven times" — the number of completeness and consecration, matching the seven sprinklings in the purification of the leper (Lev 14:7) and the Yom Kippur blood ritual (Lev 16:14, 19). **LXX:** καὶ λήμψεται Ελεαζαρ ... καὶ ῥανεῖ ἀπέναντι τοῦ προσώπου τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτῆς ἑπτάκις ("and Eleazar shall take ... and shall sprinkle before the face of the tent of testimony from her blood seven times").

**5** And-one-shall-burn [ve-saraf] [et] the-heifer [ha-parah] before-his-eyes [le-einav]; [et] her-skin [orah] and-[et] her-flesh [besarah] and-[et] her-blood [damah] with [al] her-dung [pirshah] he-shall-burn [yisrof].

> **Key terms:** The entire animal is consumed — skin, flesh, blood, and dung — nothing is salvaged. This distinguishes the red heifer from standard sacrifices where the hide goes to the priest (Lev 7:8) and certain portions are consumed. לְעֵינָיו (le-einav) = "before his eyes" — Eleazar must witness the entire burning; his visual oversight constitutes priestly supervision. The repetition of שׂרף (saraf, "to burn") — first as the qal perfect וְשָׂרַף (ve-saraf) and then as the qal imperfect יִשְׂרֹף (yisrof) — emphasizes the totality of the incineration. **LXX:** καὶ κατακαύσουσιν αὐτὴν ἐναντίον αὐτοῦ· καὶ τὸ δέρμα καὶ τὰ κρέα αὐτῆς καὶ τὸ αἷμα αὐτῆς σὺν τῇ κόπρῳ αὐτῆς κατακαυθήσεται ("and they shall burn her before him; and her skin and her flesh and her blood with her dung shall be burned").

**6** And-shall-take [ve-laqakh] the-priest [ha-kohen] wood of [ets] cedar [erez] and-hyssop [ve-ezov] and-scarlet of [u-sheni] worm [tola'at], and-shall-cast [ve-hishlikh] unto [el] the-midst of [tokh] the-burning of [srefat] the-heifer [ha-parah].

> **Key terms:** עֵץ אֶרֶז (ets erez) = "cedar wood" — the tallest and most majestic of trees. אֵזוֹב (ezov) = "hyssop" — the lowliest of shrubs (cf. 1 Kgs 5:13 [ET 4:33]). וּשְׁנִי תוֹלָעַת (u-sheni tola'at) = "and scarlet of worm" — crimson yarn dyed with the kermes insect. This triad — cedar, hyssop, scarlet — appears together in the purification of the leper (Lev 14:4, 6, 49–52) and nowhere else in Torah legislation. The cedar may represent the exalted, the hyssop the humble, and the scarlet the blood-like color that mediates between life and death. All three are cast into the burning pyre, their substances mingling with the ash of the heifer. **LXX:** καὶ λήμψεται ὁ ἱερεὺς ξύλον κέδρινον καὶ ὕσσωπον καὶ κόκκινον καὶ ἐμβαλοῦσιν εἰς μέσον τοῦ κατακαύματος τῆς δαμάλεως ("and the priest shall take cedar wood and hyssop and scarlet and they shall cast [them] into the midst of the burning of the heifer").

**7** And-shall-wash [ve-kibbes] his-garments [begadav] the-priest [ha-kohen], and-shall-bathe [ve-rakhats] his-flesh [besaro] in-water [ba-mayim], and-afterward [ve-akhar] he-shall-come [yavo] unto [el] the-camp [ha-makhaneh]; and-shall-be-unclean [ve-tamei] the-priest [ha-kohen] until [ad] the-evening [ha-arev].

> **Key terms:** The paradox begins: the priest who oversees the preparation of the purifying substance is himself rendered unclean. וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו (ve-kibbes begadav) = "and he shall wash his garments" — the standard purification protocol for contact-impurity (Lev 11:25, 28, 40; 15:5–11). וְרָחַץ בְּשָׂרוֹ בַּמַּיִם (ve-rakhats besaro ba-mayim) = "and he shall bathe his flesh in water" — full-body immersion. Despite these ablutions, he remains unclean until evening — a lesser grade of impurity than the seven-day corpse-contamination the ashes are designed to cure. **LXX:** καὶ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ὁ ἱερεὺς καὶ λούσεται τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ ὕδατι, καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα εἰσελεύσεται εἰς τὴν παρεμβολήν· καὶ ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ὁ ἱερεὺς ἕως ἑσπέρας ("and the priest shall wash his garments and bathe his body in water, and afterward he shall enter the camp; and the priest shall be unclean until evening").

**8** And-the-one-burning [ve-ha-soref] [et]-her [otah] shall-wash [yekhabbes] his-garments [begadav] in-water [ba-mayim], and-shall-bathe [ve-rakhats] [et] his-flesh [besaro] in-water [ba-mayim]; and-he-shall-be-unclean [ve-tamei] until [ad] the-evening [ha-arev].

> **Key terms:** הַשֹּׂרֵף (ha-soref) = "the one burning" — the participle identifies a distinct individual from the priest. This person, who actually tends the fire, also contracts impurity. The three-fold chain of contamination — priest (v. 7), burner (v. 8), gatherer (v. 10) — underscores the paradox: the agent of purification produces impurity in all who handle it. **LXX:** καὶ ὁ κατακαίων αὐτὴν πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ λούσεται τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ· καὶ ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας ("and the one burning her shall wash his garments and bathe his body; and he shall be unclean until evening").

**9** And-shall-gather [ve-asaf] a-man [ish] clean [tahor] [et] the-ash of [efer] the-heifer [ha-parah], and-shall-deposit [ve-hiniakh] outside [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh] in [be] a-place [maqom] clean [tahor]; and-it-shall-be [ve-hayetah] for-the-congregation of [la-adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] for-a-keeping [le-mishmeret] for-waters of [le-mei] lustration [niddah]; a-sin-offering [khattat] it [hi] [is].

> **Key terms:** אִישׁ טָהוֹר (ish tahor) = "a clean man" — a ritually pure person, distinct from both the priest and the burner. אֵפֶר (efer) = "ash" — the residue of the completely incinerated heifer, now the essential ingredient of the purification-water. בְּמָקוֹם טָהוֹר (be-maqom tahor) = "in a clean place" — the ash must be stored in a ritually pure location outside the camp, available for future use. לְמֵי נִדָּה (le-mei niddah) = "for waters of lustration/impurity" — the term נִדָּה (niddah) usually means "menstrual impurity" or "separation" (from the root נדד or נדה, "to exclude/separate"). Here it designates water that removes impurity — water of separation/purification. חַטָּאת הִוא (khattat hi) = "a sin-offering it [is]" — the red heifer is classified as a חַטָּאת (khattat), a "purification offering" or "sin offering," though it diverges radically from standard sin-offering protocol (no altar, no sanctuary, total incineration). **LXX:** καὶ συνάξει ἄνθρωπος καθαρὸς τὴν σποδὸν τῆς δαμάλεως ... εἰς ὕδωρ ῥαντισμοῦ· ἅγνισμά ἐστιν ("and a clean man shall gather the ash of the heifer ... for water of sprinkling; it is a purification").

**10** And-shall-wash [ve-kibbes] the-one-gathering [ha-osef] [et] the-ash of [efer] the-heifer [ha-parah] [et] his-garments [begadav], and-he-shall-be-unclean [ve-tamei] until [ad] the-evening [ha-arev]; and-it-shall-be [ve-hayetah] for-the-Sons of [li-venei] Israel [Yisra'el] and-for-the-sojourner [ve-la-ger] the-one-sojourning [ha-gar] in-their-midst [be-tokham] for-a-statute of [le-khuqqat] perpetuity [olam].

> **Key terms:** הָאֹסֵף (ha-osef) = "the one gathering" — the third person to contract impurity through the preparation process. וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם (ve-la-ger ha-gar be-tokham) = "and for the sojourner sojourning in their midst" — the purification law applies equally to native Israelite and resident alien, a characteristic extension of purity legislation (cf. Lev 17:8, 10, 13; Num 15:14–16). לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם (le-khuqqat olam) = "for a statute of perpetuity" — echoing the opening phrase חֻקַּת הַתּוֹרָה (v. 2) and sealing the preparation section with permanence. **LXX:** καὶ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ὁ συνάγων τὴν σποδιὰν τῆς δαμάλεως, καὶ ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας· καὶ ἔσται τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ καὶ τοῖς προσκειμένοις προσηλύτοις νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("and the one gathering the ash of the heifer shall wash his garments, and he shall be unclean until evening; and it shall be for the sons of Israel and the sojourners joined [to them] an eternal ordinance").

---

## Corpse-Contamination and the Sprinkling Ritual (vv. 11–22)

**11** The-one-touching [ha-noge'a] the-dead [be-met] of-any [le-khol] soul of [nefesh] a-human [adam] shall-be-unclean [ve-tamei] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim].

> **Key terms:** הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּמֵת לְכָל־נֶפֶשׁ אָדָם (ha-noge'a be-met le-khol-nefesh adam) = "the one touching the dead of any human soul" — the governing principle of corpse-contamination. Contact with any human corpse produces seven-day impurity — the most severe form of ritual uncleanness in the Levitical system. Unlike lesser impurities (one day), corpse-contamination requires active purification through the ash-water ritual. **LXX:** ὁ ἁπτόμενος τοῦ τεθνηκότος πάσης ψυχῆς ἀνθρώπου ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("the one touching the dead of any human soul shall be unclean seven days").

**12** He [hu] shall-purify-himself [yitkhatté] with-it [bo] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-third [ha-shelishi] and-on-the-day [u-va-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i] he-shall-be-clean [yithar]; and-if [ve-im] not [lo] he-purifies-himself [yitkhatté] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-third [ha-shelishi] and-on-the-day [u-va-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i], not [lo] he-shall-be-clean [yithar].

> **Key terms:** יִתְחַטָּא (yitkhatté) = "he shall purify himself" — the hithpael of חטא (khata'), literally "he shall de-sin himself." The hithpael form indicates reflexive action: the contaminated person must actively participate in the purification process. The double application — day three and day seven — creates a two-stage purification that cannot be compressed. If the third-day sprinkling is omitted, the seventh-day sprinkling does not suffice (Mishnah Parah 12:1). **LXX:** οὗτος ἁγνισθήσεται τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ, καὶ καθαρὸς ἔσται· ἐὰν δὲ μὴ ἀφαγνισθῇ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ, οὐ καθαρὸς ἔσται ("he shall be purified on the third day and on the seventh day, and he shall be clean; but if he is not purified on the third day and on the seventh day, he shall not be clean").

**13** Anyone [kol] the-one-touching [ha-noge'a] the-dead [be-met], in-the-soul of [be-nefesh] the-human [ha-adam] who [asher] shall-die [yamut], and-not [ve-lo] he-purifies-himself [yitkhatté], [et] the-Tabernacle of [Mishkan] YHWH he-has-made-unclean [timme]; and-shall-be-cut-off [ve-nikhretah] the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-that [ha-hi] from-Israel [mi-Yisra'el]; for [ki] the-waters of [mei] lustration [niddah] not [lo] was-sprinkled [zorraq] upon-him [alav], unclean [tamei] he-shall-be [yihyeh]; still [od] his-uncleanness [tum'ato] [is] upon-him [bo].

> **Key terms:** אֶת־מִשְׁכַּן יְהוָה טִמֵּא (et-Mishkan YHWH timme) = "the Tabernacle of YHWH he has made unclean" — the unpurified person's corpse-contamination radiates outward, defiling the sanctuary even at a distance. This concept — that impurity assaults the sanctuary — is central to the priestly theology of Leviticus and Numbers (Lev 15:31; 20:3; Num 19:20). וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל (ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-Yisra'el) = "and that soul shall be cut off from Israel" — the כָּרֵת (karet) penalty, divine excision from the community. זֹרַּק (zorraq) = "was sprinkled" — the pual (passive) of זרק (zaraq, "to throw/toss/sprinkle"). עוֹד טֻמְאָתוֹ בּוֹ (od tum'ato bo) = "still his uncleanness is upon him" — emphatic final clause underscoring that unresolved corpse-contamination persists indefinitely. **LXX:** πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος τοῦ τεθνηκότος ... τὴν σκηνὴν κυρίου ἐμίανεν· ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἐξ Ισραηλ ("everyone touching the dead ... has defiled the tent of [the] Lord; that soul shall be utterly destroyed from Israel").

**14** This [zot] [is] the-Torah [ha-Torah]: a-human [adam] when [ki] he-dies [yamut] in-a-tent [be-ohel], everyone [kol] the-one-coming [ha-ba] unto [el] the-tent [ha-ohel] and-everyone [ve-khol] who [asher] [is] in-the-tent [ba-ohel] shall-be-unclean [yitma] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim].

> **Key terms:** אָדָם כִּי־יָמוּת בְּאֹהֶל (adam ki-yamut be-ohel) = "a human when he dies in a tent" — the governing case for tent-contamination. Not only direct contact but mere presence in the same enclosed space with a corpse produces seven-day impurity. כָּל־הַבָּא אֶל־הָאֹהֶל (kol-ha-ba el-ha-ohel) = "everyone coming into the tent" — those who enter the tent after the death also contract impurity. This extends contamination beyond touch to spatial proximity within an enclosure. **LXX:** οὗτος ὁ νόμος· ἄνθρωπος ἐὰν ἀποθάνῃ ἐν οἰκίᾳ, πᾶς ὁ εἰσπορευόμενος εἰς τὴν οἰκίαν ... ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("this is the law: if a person dies in a house, everyone entering the house ... shall be unclean seven days") — notably rendering אֹהֶל ("tent") as οἰκία ("house"), reflecting the LXX translators' settled-land context.

**15** And-every [ve-khol] vessel [keli] open [patuakh], which [asher] there-is-no [ein] a-cover [tsamid] of-a-seal [patil] upon-it [alav], unclean [tamei] it [hu] [is].

> **Key terms:** כְּלִי פָתוּחַ (keli fatuakh) = "an open vessel" — an unsealed container within the death-tent absorbs impurity through its opening. צָמִיד פָּתִיל (tsamid patil) = "a cover of a seal" — literally "a bound/fastened cord-covering." Only a tightly sealed vessel — one with a secured lid — remains clean. The principle is that impurity, like a miasma, enters through openings. This has practical implications: food stores, water containers, and other vessels must be sealed in the presence of a corpse. **LXX:** καὶ πᾶν σκεῦος ἀνεῳγμένον, ὅσα οὐχὶ δεσμὸν καταδέδεται ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ, ἀκάθαρτά ἐστιν ("and every open vessel, which does not have a binding tied upon it, is unclean").

**16** And-everyone [ve-khol] who [asher] shall-touch [yigga] upon [al] the-face of [penei] the-field [ha-sadeh] a-slain-one of [ba-khalal] a-sword [kherev], or [o] a-dead-one [be-met], or [o] a-bone of [be-etsem] a-human [adam], or [o] a-grave [be-qaver], shall-be-unclean [yitma] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim].

> **Key terms:** עַל־פְּנֵי הַשָּׂדֶה (al-penei ha-sadeh) = "upon the face of the field" — extending corpse-contamination beyond the enclosed tent to the open field. Four categories of contamination: (1) חֲלַל־חֶרֶב (khalal-kherev) = "a slain-one of a sword" — one killed in battle; (2) מֵת (met) = "a dead one" — a corpse from any cause; (3) עֶצֶם אָדָם (etsem adam) = "a human bone" — even skeletal remains transmit impurity; (4) קֶבֶר (qever) = "a grave" — contact with a burial site itself. This fourfold enumeration establishes that corpse-contamination is comprehensive: not just fresh bodies but old bones and graves convey impurity. **LXX:** καὶ πᾶς ὃς ἐὰν ἅψηται ἐπὶ προσώπου τοῦ πεδίου τραυματίου ἢ νεκροῦ ἢ ὀστέου ἀνθρωπίνου ἢ μνήματος, ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("and everyone who touches upon the face of the plain a wounded one or a dead one or a human bone or a tomb shall be unclean seven days").

**17** And-they-shall-take [ve-laqkhu] for-the-unclean [la-tamei] from-the-dust of [me-afar] the-burning of [srefat] the-sin-offering [ha-khattat], and-one-shall-put [ve-natan] upon-it [alav] living [khayyim] water [mayim] unto [el] a-vessel [keli].

> **Key terms:** מֵעֲפַר שְׂרֵפַת הַחַטָּאת (me-afar srefat ha-khattat) = "from the dust of the burning of the sin-offering" — the ash from the red heifer (v. 9) is here called עָפָר (afar, "dust") rather than אֵפֶר (efer, "ash"), perhaps emphasizing its earth-like, powdery quality. מַיִם חַיִּים (mayim khayyim) = "living water" — fresh, flowing water (spring water), not stagnant or collected water. The juxtaposition of the ash of death with "living water" crystallizes the chapter's theology: death's contamination is overcome by the union of sacrificial residue and living water. **LXX:** καὶ λήμψονται τῷ ἀκαθάρτῳ ἀπὸ τῆς σποδιᾶς τῆς κατακεκαυμένης τοῦ ἁγνισμοῦ, καὶ ἐκχεοῦσιν ἐπ᾽ αὐτὴν ὕδωρ ζῶν εἰς σκεῦος ("and they shall take for the unclean [one] from the ash of the burned purification, and they shall pour upon it living water into a vessel").

**18** And-shall-take [ve-laqakh] hyssop [ezov] and-shall-dip [ve-taval] in-the-water [ba-mayim] a-man [ish] clean [tahor], and-shall-sprinkle [ve-hizzah] upon [al] the-tent [ha-ohel] and-upon [ve-al] all [kol] the-vessels [ha-kelim] and-upon [ve-al] the-souls [ha-nefashot] who [asher] were [hayu] there [sham]; and-upon [ve-al] the-one-touching [ha-noge'a] the-bone [ba-etsem] or [o] the-slain-one [va-khalal] or [o] the-dead-one [va-met] or [o] the-grave [va-qaver].

> **Key terms:** אֵזוֹב (ezov) = "hyssop" — the same plant cast into the pyre (v. 6) now serves as the instrument of sprinkling, connecting preparation and application. אִישׁ טָהוֹר (ish tahor) = "a clean man" — as in v. 9, a ritually pure person must administer the purification. The sprinkling covers everything contaminated: the tent (the space), the vessels (the objects), the persons (the souls), and those who touched specific sources of contamination (bone, slain, dead, grave) — recapitulating the categories of v. 16. **LXX:** καὶ λήμψεται ὕσσωπον καὶ βάψει εἰς τὸ ὕδωρ ἀνὴρ καθαρός, καὶ περιρρανεῖ ἐπὶ τὸν οἶκον καὶ ἐπὶ τὰ σκεύη ... ("and a clean man shall take hyssop and dip [it] in the water, and shall sprinkle upon the house and upon the vessels ...").

**19** And-shall-sprinkle [ve-hizzah] the-clean [ha-tahor] upon [al] the-unclean [ha-tamei] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-third [ha-shelishi] and-on-the-day [u-va-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i]; and-he-shall-purify-him [ve-khitte'o] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i], and-he-shall-wash [ve-kibbes] his-garments [begadav] and-shall-bathe [ve-rakhats] in-water [ba-mayim], and-he-shall-be-clean [ve-taher] in-the-evening [ba-arev].

> **Key terms:** וְחִטְּאוֹ (ve-khitte'o) = "and he shall purify him" — the piel of חטא (khata') with third-person suffix, meaning "to purify/de-sin him." The seventh-day sprinkling completes the purification, after which the standard washing-and-bathing protocol applies. The restored person is clean by evening of the seventh day — a full week's process. **LXX:** καὶ περιρρανεῖ ὁ καθαρὸς ἐπὶ τὸν ἀκάθαρτον ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ καὶ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ ... καὶ καθαρὸς ἔσται τὸ ἑσπέρας ("and the clean [one] shall sprinkle upon the unclean on the third day and on the seventh day ... and he shall be clean at evening").

**20** And-a-man [ve-ish] who [asher] shall-be-unclean [yitma] and-not [ve-lo] shall-purify-himself [yitkhatté], and-shall-be-cut-off [ve-nikhretah] the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-that [ha-hi] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-assembly [ha-qahal]; for [ki] [et] the-sanctuary of [miqdash] YHWH he-has-made-unclean [timme]; the-waters of [mei] lustration [niddah] not [lo] was-sprinkled [zorraq] upon-him [alav]; unclean [tamei] he [hu] [is].

> **Key terms:** מִתּוֹךְ הַקָּהָל (mi-tokh ha-qahal) = "from the midst of the assembly" — v. 13 says "from Israel" (מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל); here "from the assembly" (מִתּוֹךְ הַקָּהָל). The variation sharpens the communal dimension: the impure person is excised from the worshiping congregation. מִקְדַּשׁ יְהוָה (miqdash YHWH) = "the sanctuary of YHWH" — where v. 13 uses מִשְׁכַּן (Mishkan, "Tabernacle"), v. 20 uses מִקְדָּשׁ (miqdash, "sanctuary"). Both terms denote the same sacred space, but the variation emphasizes its holiness (from קדשׁ, q-d-sh, "holy"). **LXX:** καὶ ἄνθρωπος ὃς ἐὰν μιανθῇ καὶ μὴ ἀφαγνισθῇ, ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἐκ μέσου τῆς συναγωγῆς, ὅτι τὰ ἅγια κυρίου ἐμίανεν ("and a person who is defiled and is not purified, that soul shall be utterly destroyed from the midst of the assembly, because he has defiled the holy things of [the] Lord").

**21** And-it-shall-be [ve-hayetah] for-them [lahem] for-a-statute of [le-khuqqat] perpetuity [olam]; and-the-one-sprinkling [u-mazzeh] the-waters of [mei] the-lustration [ha-niddah] shall-wash [yekhabbes] his-garments [begadav]; and-the-one-touching [ve-ha-noge'a] the-waters of [be-mei] the-lustration [ha-niddah] shall-be-unclean [yitma] until [ad] the-evening [ha-arev].

> **Key terms:** וּמַזֵּה מֵי הַנִּדָּה (u-mazzeh mei ha-niddah) = "and the one sprinkling the waters of lustration" — the hiphil participle of נזה (nazah, "to sprinkle"). Even the one who administers the purification water contracts a lesser impurity (until evening), extending the paradox: the purification agent contaminates its handler. וְהַנֹּגֵעַ בְּמֵי הַנִּדָּה (ve-ha-noge'a be-mei ha-niddah) = "and the one touching the waters of lustration" — not just the sprinkler but anyone who touches the water becomes unclean. The purifying substance is itself a source of contamination to the clean. **LXX:** καὶ ἔσται αὐτοῖς νόμιμον αἰώνιον· καὶ ὁ περιρραίνων ὕδωρ ῥαντισμοῦ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ, καὶ ὁ ἁπτόμενος τοῦ ὕδατος τοῦ ῥαντισμοῦ ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας ("and it shall be for them an eternal ordinance; and the one sprinkling the water of sprinkling shall wash his garments, and the one touching the water of sprinkling shall be unclean until evening").

**22** And-everything [ve-khol] which [asher] shall-touch [yigga] in-him [bo] the-unclean [ha-tamei] shall-be-unclean [yitma]; and-the-soul [ve-ha-nefesh] the-one-touching [ha-noga'at] shall-be-unclean [titma] until [ad] the-evening [ha-arev].

> **Key terms:** The chapter closes with the principle of secondary contamination: whatever the corpse-contaminated person touches becomes unclean, and whoever touches that contaminated person contracts impurity until evening. This is a chain of diminishing impurity — the original contamination lasts seven days (v. 11), but the secondary contamination lasts only until evening. הַנֹּגַעַת (ha-noga'at) = "the one touching" — feminine participle, agreeing with נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, feminine noun), not indicating a specifically female subject. **LXX:** καὶ πᾶν ὅσον ἐὰν ἅψηται αὐτοῦ ὁ ἀκάθαρτος, ἀκάθαρτον ἔσται· καὶ ἡ ψυχὴ ἡ ἁπτομένη ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας ("and everything that the unclean [one] touches shall be unclean; and the soul that touches [it] shall be unclean until evening").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 171–172

1. **Parashah marker:** An open section marker (פ) precedes 19:1 in the Leningrad Codex, marking the beginning of פָּרָשַׁת חֻקַּת (Parashat Khuqqat). This is one of the most well-known parashah divisions, as the red heifer ritual opens a self-contained legislative unit distinct from the Korah rebellion narrative that precedes it (Num 16–18).

2. **Setumah within the chapter:** A closed section marker (ס) appears between v. 10 and v. 11, dividing the chapter into its two natural halves: the preparation of the ash (vv. 1–10) and the laws of corpse-contamination and their remedy (vv. 11–22). This division is reflected in the structure of the translation above.

3. **The phrase חֻקַּת הַתּוֹרָה (khuqqat ha-Torah, v. 2):** This compound expression occurs only here and in Num 31:21 in the entire Hebrew Bible. The more common formulation is חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (khuqqat olam, "statute of perpetuity"). The unique phrase "statute of the Torah" may elevate this particular law above other statutes, marking it as paradigmatic of Torah legislation — a law whose authority rests on divine command rather than human reason.

4. **Vocalization of פָּרָה (parah, "heifer"):** The Leningrad Codex consistently vocalizes the word with qamets under the pe and patakh under the resh, confirming the standard reading. The word occurs five times in the chapter (vv. 2, 5, 6, 9, 10), always with the definite article after the first occurrence.

5. **The double verb in v. 5 — וְשָׂרַף ... יִשְׂרֹף (ve-saraf ... yisrof):** The shift from ve-qatal (ve-saraf, "and-one-shall-burn") to the yiqtol (yisrof, "he shall burn") within the same verse is preserved clearly in the manuscript. This syntactic shift may indicate a move from narrative sequence to emphatic elaboration: "and one shall burn the heifer ... her skin and her flesh and her blood with her dung he shall burn."

6. **מֵי נִדָּה (mei niddah, "waters of lustration/impurity"):** This phrase occurs four times in the chapter (vv. 9, 13, 20, 21) and is one of the most debated terms in Levitical legislation. The Leningrad vocalization consistently reads נִדָּה with a dagesh in the dalet and qamets-he at the end, confirming the standard form. The word's semantic range — from menstrual separation (Lev 15:19–33) to purification water (Num 19) — suggests a core meaning of "that which removes" or "that which separates," functioning differently depending on context.

### LXX Divergences (Codex Vaticanus)

7. **δάμαλις vs. פָּרָה:** The LXX consistently renders פָּרָה (parah, "cow/heifer") as δάμαλις (damalis, "young cow/heifer") rather than βοῦς (bous, "cow/ox"), specifying a younger animal. The Hebrew פָּרָה does not inherently specify age; the Greek narrows the semantic range.

8. **ἅγνισμα for חַטָּאת (v. 9):** Where the MT classifies the red heifer as a חַטָּאת (khattat, "sin-offering/purification-offering"), the Vaticanus LXX renders it ἅγνισμα ("purification"), emphasizing the cleansing function rather than the sin-offering category. This interpretive choice reflects the translators' recognition that the red heifer does not follow standard sin-offering protocol.

9. **οἰκία for אֹהֶל (v. 14):** The Vaticanus renders אֹהֶל (ohel, "tent") as οἰκία (oikia, "house") in v. 14, reflecting the settled Greek-speaking context of the translators. The Hebrew preserves the wilderness setting; the Greek adapts to post-settlement reality.

10. **ῥαντισμός for נִדָּה:** Throughout the chapter, the LXX renders מֵי נִדָּה (mei niddah) as ὕδωρ ῥαντισμοῦ (hydor rhantismou, "water of sprinkling"), interpreting the function (sprinkling) rather than translating the Hebrew term (separation/impurity). This is an interpretive rather than literal rendering, and it avoids the potential confusion of associating purification-water with menstrual impurity.

### Theological and Structural Observations

11. **The paradox of purification and contamination:** The chapter's central paradox — that the purification agent contaminates those who prepare it (vv. 7–8, 10, 21) while purifying those who receive it (vv. 12, 19) — is without parallel in Levitical legislation. The red heifer ash makes the clean unclean and the unclean clean. The rabbis identified this as the supreme example of a חֹק (khoq), a divine statute beyond human understanding (Bemidbar Rabbah 19:3: "Even Solomon, wisest of all, said of this, 'I thought I would acquire wisdom, but it is far from me' [Eccl 7:23]").

12. **The triad of cedar, hyssop, and scarlet (v. 6):** This same combination appears in the purification of the leper (Lev 14:4, 6, 49–52), creating an intertextual link between corpse-contamination and the "living death" of leprosy (צָרַעַת, tsara'at). Both conditions represent extreme forms of impurity requiring extraordinary purification rituals conducted outside the camp.

13. **Living water (מַיִם חַיִּים, mayim khayyim, v. 17):** The same term appears in the leper purification (Lev 14:5–6), the jealousy ordeal (Num 5:17), and descriptions of springs and wells (Gen 26:19; Jer 2:13; 17:13; Zech 14:8). The pairing of "living water" with the ash of death creates a potent symbolic antithesis: life overcoming death, flowing vitality dissolving the contamination of mortality.

14. **Structural chiasm of the chapter:**
    - A: Divine command to Moses and Aaron (v. 1)
    - B: Preparation of the heifer — outside the camp (vv. 2–6)
    - C: Contamination of the preparers (vv. 7–10)
    - C': Contamination from the dead (vv. 11–16)
    - B': Application of the ash-water (vv. 17–19)
    - A': Penalty for non-compliance and final contamination law (vv. 20–22)

    The center (C/C') juxtaposes the impurity of the preparers with the impurity of the corpse-contaminated — both are unclean, but for opposite reasons: one from handling holiness, the other from contacting death.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio 171r–172r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pages 126–127. All transliterations follow the EMET standard. YHWH is transliterated, not translated. Conjunctions are preserved. Numbers rendered in English words.*
